era_context: The Ming Dynasty is in the final year of the Yongle Emperor's reign (r. 1402–1424). The Hussite Wars are in their early phase in Bohemia. The Ottoman Empire is consolidating power in Anatolia and the Balkans. The Portuguese are beginning systematic exploration of the African coast under Prince Henry the Navigator.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica and South America (no contemporary written records)
Northern and Eastern Europe outside major political centers

events.title: Death of the Yongle Emperor
events.description: The Yongle Emperor, one of the most powerful and expansionist Ming rulers, died in 1424 (some sources give 1424, others 1425; the exact date is debated). His reign (1402–1424) was marked by military campaigns, the construction of the Forbidden City, the compilation of the Yongle Encyclopedia, and the sponsorship of Zheng He's treasure voyages. His death marked a transition in Ming policy, with subsequent rulers being less expansionist and more focused on consolidation and defense.
events.certainty_note: The Yongle Emperor's death is well-documented in Ming records, though the exact date (1424 vs. 1425) varies by source.

events.title: Hussite Wars: Early Phase in Bohemia
events.description: The Hussite Wars, sparked by the execution of Jan Hus in 1415, were in their early and most radical phase in 1424. The Taborite faction, a radical Hussite group, was engaged in military campaigns against Catholic forces and rival Hussite factions. The year 1424 saw significant military activity, including the Battle of Malešov. These wars were driven by religious reform demands and social upheaval, and they would reshape Central European religious and political landscapes.
key_figures: Prokop the Great (Hussite commander, active from 1424)

sources.name: Laurentius of Březová, Chronicle of the Hussite Wars

events.certainty_note: Contemporary chronicles document the Hussite Wars in detail. The death of Jan Žižka in 1424 is well-attested.
cross_references: 1434-001 (Battle of Lipany, end of radical Hussite phase)

events.title: Death of Jan Žižka, Hussite Military Commander
events.description: Jan Žižka, the legendary Hussite military commander, died in 1424 (some sources give October 1424). Žižka was blind but led Hussite forces to numerous victories against Catholic armies. His death marked a transition in Hussite military leadership, with Prokop the Great assuming command. Žižka's innovations in military tactics, particularly the use of wagon fortifications, influenced European warfare for centuries.

sources.name: Laurentius of Březová, Chronicle of the Hussite Wars

events.certainty_note: Žižka's death in 1424 is well-documented in contemporary sources.

events.title: Ottoman Consolidation Under Murad II
events.description: Murad II, who became Ottoman Sultan in 1421, was consolidating Ottoman power in Anatolia and the Balkans in 1424. His reign saw the suppression of internal rebellions and the expansion of Ottoman control over former Byzantine and Serbian territories. Murad II's military and administrative reforms strengthened the Ottoman state, making it a major regional power.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman consolidation under Murad II is well-documented in multiple contemporary sources.

events.title: Portuguese Exploration of the African Coast
events.description: Portuguese expeditions under Prince Henry the Navigator continued systematic exploration of the West African coast in 1424. These voyages were motivated by trade, gold, and religious conversion. By 1424, Portuguese knowledge of African geography was expanding, though major breakthroughs like the crossing of Cape Bojador would come later (1434).

sources.name: Gomes Eanes de Zurara, Chronicle of the Discovery of Guinea

events.certainty_note: The exact timeline of early Portuguese exploration is uncertain. Most detailed accounts come from later chronicles.
cross_references: 1488-001 (Bartolomeu Dias rounds Cape of Good Hope)

disconfirming_evidence: The Yongle Emperor's death date is disputed in sources: some give 1424, others 1425. The significance of his reign has been reinterpreted by recent scholarship, which emphasizes the costs of his military campaigns and the eventual curtailment of his expansionist policies by his successors. Jan Žižka's legendary status has been somewhat mythologized; while his military innovations were significant, some accounts exaggerate his personal role and underestimate the contributions of other Hussite commanders. The Portuguese exploration narrative has been complicated by postcolonial scholarship, which notes that early Portuguese expeditions were often unsuccessful and that the 'Age of Exploration' framing obscures the agency and sophistication of African societies.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1424 is similar to previous years: heavily weighted toward East Asia and Europe, with minimal coverage of Africa, the Americas, and other regions. Chinese sources are abundant but retrospective. European sources are contemporary but elite-focused. The 'significance' of events reflects historiographic bias toward literate, centralized states. The death of the Yongle Emperor is a major event in Chinese history but may be less significant globally than the historiographic record suggests.

graph_edges.note: Yongle Emperor's death led to succession crisis and transition to Xuande Emperor
Jan Žižka's death occurred during the Hussite Wars
Žižka's death led to Prokop the Great's command and continuation of Hussite Wars
Ottoman consolidation under Murad II led to the fall of Constantinople in 1453
